Tortuosity Explorer — Condition 1 (60° gratings 4Hz)

Strain: jfrc100_es_shibire_kir Files: 30 Flies (passed QC): 653 Excluded (QC): 177 Window sizes: 0.5s, 1.0s, 2.0s, 3.0s, 5.0s, 7.0s Arena radius: 119.0 mm

QC: excluded flies with mean forward velocity < 3 mm/s or mean distance from centre > 110 mm during stimulus.

What is tortuosity?

Tortuosity measures how winding a trajectory is over a given time window. It is computed as:

tortuosity = path length / displacement

where path length is the total distance walked along the trajectory within the window, and displacement is the straight-line distance between the start and end positions of that window.

A perfectly straight path has a tortuosity of 1.0. Higher values indicate more winding trajectories — a fly walking in a tight circle would have very high tortuosity, while one walking in a gentle arc would be closer to 1.

Straight path (tortuosity ≈ 1.0): Winding path (tortuosity >> 1): A ————————> B A ~~> · path ≈ displacement · <~~· · ~~> B path >> displacement

Examples from this dataset (5s window):

Path lengthDisplacementTortuosity
Straightest 5s segment70.9 mm69.7 mm1.02
Most winding 5s segment67.5 mm2.3 mm29.62

The window size controls the timescale over which tortuosity is evaluated. Short windows (0.5–1s) capture individual turns. Longer windows (3–7s) capture extended looping behaviour. Use the slider on each plot to explore different timescales.

The three trajectory subplots below show the same fly with tortuosity computed at three different window sizes (1s, 3s, 7s). Dark colours (low tortuosity) indicate straight segments; bright colours (high tortuosity) indicate winding or looping segments. Use the dropdown to switch between example flies.

Example Trajectory

B. Mean Tortuosity by Radial Zone

What is the effective turning radius?

The effective turning radius measures the spatial scale of a fly’s turns. It is derived from full rotation events — segments of the trajectory where the fly’s heading has accumulated 360° of change in one direction.

effective radius = path length per 360° / (2π)

This is the radius of a circle that would produce the same path length for one full revolution:

Large sweeping loop Tight pivot (high effective radius) (low effective radius) . - . . / \ / \ | r | ← path is long | r| ← path is short \ / for 360° \ / for 360° ' - ' ' eff. radius = large eff. radius = small (e.g. 20–50 mm) (e.g. 1–5 mm)

Hypothesis: If flies adjust their turning behaviour based on proximity to the arena wall, we would expect to see smaller effective turning radii near the edge (tight turns to avoid the wall) and larger radii in the centre (more space for sweeping loops). This would appear as a leftward shift in the outer-zone distribution relative to the inner zone.

In the histogram below, effective turning radii are grouped by the same distance zones as Plot A (coloured lines = stimulus, grey = baseline). Rotations are classified by their midpoint frame. CW and CCW rotations are combined. Dashed vertical lines show the median for each distribution. Rotations overlapping with the middle 5s around the stimulus direction change are excluded. The x-axis uses a logarithmic scale.
